schedule.description: 5 weekday sessions of ~2 hours each. Monday–Wednesday focus on lectures/theory, Thursday on exercises/practice, Friday on reading and connections to loss landscapes.

resources.name: Visual Differential Geometry and Forms – Tristan Needham
Do Carmo – Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces
Lee – Introduction to Smooth Manifolds (2nd ed.)
Absil et al. – Optimization Algorithms on Matrix Manifolds
Li et al. – Visualizing the Loss Landscape of Neural Nets (2018)
Amari – Information Geometry and Its Applications
Sagun et al. – Empirical Analysis of the Hessian of Over-Parametrized NNs
Dauphin et al. – Identifying and Attacking the Saddle Point Problem (2014)
Geometry of Neural Network Loss Surfaces via Random Matrix Theory (Pennington & Worah 2017)
Eigencurve – Measuring Curvature of the Loss Landscape
Topological Properties of the Loss Landscape (Ballard et al.)
Frederick & Bhatt – A Physics Perspective on Loss Landscape

phases.title: Mathematical Foundations
phases.description: Rebuild the core mathematical fluency you'll need for everything ahead: linear algebra (eigenvalues, quadratic forms, spectral decomposition), multivariable calculus (gradients, Jacobians, Hessians), and just enough topology (open sets, continuity, compactness) to read manifold definitions comfortably. This phase moves fast because you already have CS intuition — we're formalising what you half-know.
phases.bookChapters: Needham, Visual Differential Geometry and Forms — Ch. 1–3 (Euclidean Geometry, revisiting vectors and transformations)
phases.bookDescription: Needham gives a beautifully visual, intuition-first treatment. Read chapters 1–3 as a parallel track — they reframe linear algebra and calculus in the geometric language you'll use for the rest of the plan.
phases.completionNote: You can confidently compute eigendecompositions, Hessians, and Taylor expansions in multiple dimensions. You understand what 'open set', 'homeomorphism', and 'continuity' mean topologically. You can explain why the Hessian of a loss function at a critical point tells you about local curvature.

weeks.title: Linear Algebra Revival: Eigenvalues, Quadratic Forms, Spectral Theorem

slots.label: Session 1 — Linear Maps & Eigenvalues
slots.description:
Watch 3Blue1Brown's **Essence of Linear Algebra** chapters 1–7 (linear transformations through eigenvalues). Focus on the geometric interpretation: eigenvectors are directions preserved by the transformation, eigenvalues are how much they stretch.

**Connection:** In a neural network's loss landscape, the Hessian matrix at a critical point is a linear map. Its eigenvalues tell you about curvature — positive means a valley, negative means a ridge, zero means a flat direction.

links.text: Ch 1: Vectors, what even are they?

subtasks.label: Watch 3B1B chapters 1–4 (vectors, span, transformations, matrix multiplication)
Watch 3B1B chapters 5–7 (determinant, inverse, column space, null space)
Write down 3 sentences explaining eigenvalues geometrically in your own words

slots.label: Session 2 — Spectral Decomposition & Quadratic Forms
slots.description:
Watch 3B1B eigen chapters (13–14), then study the **spectral theorem for symmetric matrices**: any real symmetric matrix can be decomposed as Q Λ Qᵀ. Then study quadratic forms: x^T A x, and how eigenvalues determine whether the quadratic form is positive definite, negative definite, or indefinite.

**Connection:** The Hessian of a loss function is symmetric. Its spectral decomposition gives you the principal curvature directions. Positive definite Hessian = local minimum. Indefinite = saddle point. This is the core geometric content of loss landscape analysis.

links.text: MIT OCW 18.06: Symmetric Matrices & Positive Definiteness

subtasks.label: Watch 3B1B chapters 13–14 (change of basis, eigenvectors)
Read/watch spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices
Work through: classify x^T A x for 3 example matrices by computing eigenvalues
Write down the connection between Hessian eigenvalues and critical point type

slots.label: Session 3 — SVD and Matrix Norms
slots.description:
Study the **Singular Value Decomposition** (SVD): every matrix A = U Σ Vᵀ. Understand singular values as the semi-axes of the ellipsoid that A maps the unit sphere to. Study Frobenius norm, operator norm, and their relation to singular values.

**Connection:** SVD appears everywhere in NN interpretability — weight matrix decomposition, low-rank approximations, and the filter normalisation used in Li et al.'s loss landscape visualisation paper.

subtasks.label: Watch Steve Brunton's SVD lecture
Compute SVD of a 3×2 matrix by hand
Understand: how SVD relates to eigendecomposition for symmetric matrices

slots.label: Session 4 — Coding Linear Algebra
slots.description:
Implement key concepts in Python/NumPy. This cements your understanding and connects the math to your ML workflow.

- Compute eigenvalues/eigenvectors of a random symmetric matrix
- Verify the spectral theorem: reconstruct A from Q Λ Qᵀ
- Classify random quadratic forms as pos. def / neg. def / indefinite
- Compute SVD and verify A = U Σ Vᵀ

subtasks.label: Implement eigendecomposition and verify spectral theorem
Write a function to classify a symmetric matrix (pos def, neg def, indefinite, semidefinite)
Compute SVD and reconstruct the original matrix
Compute the Hessian of f(x,y) = x² + 3xy + y² at (0,0) and classify the critical point

slots.label: Session 5 — Needham Ch. 1: Euclidean Geometry Revisited
slots.description:
Read Needham's **Visual Differential Geometry and Forms**, Chapter 1. This reframes basic geometric ideas (angles, distances, transformations) in a way that builds toward differential geometry. Needham writes for visual thinkers — perfect for a CS background.

Take notes on any concepts that feel new or that you'd explain differently than a typical textbook.

subtasks.label: Read Needham Ch. 1 (first half)
Read Needham Ch. 1 (second half)
Write a half-page summary of key geometric ideas from the chapter

weeks.title: Multivariable Calculus: Gradients, Jacobians, Hessians, Taylor Expansion

slots.label: Session 1 — Gradients and Directional Derivatives
slots.description:
Study the **gradient** as the direction of steepest ascent, **directional derivatives**, and the geometric meaning of the gradient being perpendicular to level sets.

**Connection:** Gradient descent literally follows −∇L. Understanding that ∇L is perpendicular to the loss contour at every point is foundational for understanding optimisation trajectories geometrically.

subtasks.label: Watch 3B1B or Khan gradient/directional derivative videos
Prove: gradient is perpendicular to level sets (follow the proof in notes)
Compute gradients and directional derivatives for 3 example functions

slots.label: Session 2 — Jacobians and the Chain Rule
slots.description:
Study the **Jacobian matrix** as the best linear approximation to a map f: ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ. The multivariable chain rule says J(g∘f) = J(g) · J(f) — matrix multiplication!

**Connection:** Backpropagation is literally the chain rule applied layer by layer. The Jacobian of each layer composes. Understanding this geometrically (each layer's Jacobian stretches/rotates the tangent space) is key to understanding how gradients flow through deep networks.

subtasks.label: Watch Jacobian lectures
Compute Jacobians for 3 maps (e.g. polar coordinates, simple neural net layer)
Verify chain rule: compute J(g∘f) directly and as J(g)·J(f)
Write down how backpropagation is the chain rule in Jacobian form

slots.label: Session 3 — Hessians and Multivariable Taylor Expansion
slots.description:
Study the **Hessian** H(f) as the matrix of second partial derivatives. The second-order Taylor expansion is f(x+h) ≈ f(x) + ∇f·h + ½ hᵀ H h. The Hessian captures the **curvature** of f at a point.

**Connection:** Near a critical point of the loss L (where ∇L = 0), the loss is approximately L(θ+δ) ≈ L(θ) + ½ δᵀ H δ. The Hessian's eigenvalues determine the curvature in each principal direction. Large positive eigenvalues = sharp minima. Small eigenvalues = flat directions. This is the basis of the 'sharp vs flat minima' debate in deep learning.

links.text: Khan Academy: Second partial derivatives & Hessian

subtasks.label: Watch Hessian / second-order Taylor lectures
Compute Hessians for 3 functions and classify critical points
Write the quadratic approximation of a 2D loss near a critical point
Understand: what makes a minimum 'sharp' vs 'flat' in Hessian terms

slots.label: Session 4 — Hessian Computation in PyTorch
slots.description:
Use PyTorch autograd to compute gradients and Hessians of small neural networks. Visualise loss surfaces of toy 2D problems.

- Train a tiny MLP on a 2D toy dataset
- Compute the full Hessian at the trained weights using torch.autograd.functional.hessian
- Compute eigenvalues of the Hessian
- Plot the loss surface in 2D and overlay eigenvectors at the minimum

subtasks.label: Train a tiny MLP (2 hidden units) on a 2D dataset
Compute full Hessian at trained weights
Compute and plot eigenvalue spectrum of the Hessian
Plot 2D loss surface and overlay principal curvature directions

slots.label: Session 5 — Needham Ch. 2 & Intro Topology
slots.description:
Read Needham Ch. 2, then spend 45 min on basic **point-set topology**: open sets, closed sets, continuity as preimage-of-open-is-open, compactness, connectedness. You don't need to go deep — just enough to read 'let M be a smooth manifold' without panic.

Recommended: Munkres Ch. 2 §12–17 or any topology primer notes.

subtasks.label: Read Needham Ch. 2
Read a topology primer: open/closed sets, continuity, compactness (~45 min)
Write down definitions of: open set, continuous map, homeomorphism, compact set

weeks.title: Implicit Function Theorem, Level Sets, and Parametric Surfaces

slots.label: Session 1 — Implicit Function Theorem
slots.description:
Study the **Implicit Function Theorem** (IFT): if F(x,y) = 0 and ∂F/∂y ≠ 0, then locally y = g(x). The IFT is the rigorous foundation for 'level sets are smooth curves/surfaces'. Also study the **Inverse Function Theorem**.

**Connection:** The loss level sets {θ : L(θ) = c} are implicitly defined surfaces in parameter space. The IFT guarantees they are smooth manifolds (when ∇L ≠ 0). Understanding this is key to thinking about loss landscapes geometrically.

subtasks.label: Study the statement and geometric meaning of the Implicit Function Theorem
Work through 2 examples of applying IFT
Study the Inverse Function Theorem and its relation to IFT

slots.label: Session 2 — Parametric Curves and Surfaces
slots.description:
Study **parametric representations** of curves γ(t) and surfaces σ(u,v). Understand the difference between a parametrisation and the geometric object itself. Study regular parametrisations (non-vanishing tangent/Jacobian).

This is your first encounter with the idea that will become 'charts on a manifold': a parametrisation is a map from ℝⁿ into a geometric object.

subtasks.label: Study parametric curves: γ(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t)), tangent vectors, arc length
Study parametric surfaces: σ(u,v), tangent plane, normal vector
Parametrise: sphere, torus, and graph of a function z = f(x,y)

slots.label: Session 3 — Level Sets as Manifolds
slots.description:
Bring together IFT + parametrisation: a level set F(x) = c, when ∇F ≠ 0, is a smooth manifold that can be locally parametrised. Study the tangent space to a level set: it's the kernel of the gradient.

**Connection:** The loss level sets {θ : L(θ) = c} form a foliation of parameter space. The gradient ∇L is perpendicular to each level set. Gradient descent crosses level sets; the geometry of these level sets determines how optimization behaves.

links.text: MIT OCW 18.02: Level curves and surfaces

subtasks.label: Prove: tangent space to a level set = ker(∇F)
Visualise level sets of simple 2D functions and their gradients
Write a paragraph connecting loss level sets to gradient descent trajectories

slots.label: Session 4 — Visualising Level Sets and Gradient Flows
slots.description:
Code up visualisations:
- Plot level sets of f(x,y) for several functions
- Overlay gradient vectors (arrows perpendicular to contours)
- Simulate gradient descent on a 2D loss surface and overlay the trajectory on the contour plot
- Visualise how trajectory crosses level sets

subtasks.label: Plot contours and gradient field for 3 functions
Implement gradient descent on a 2D function and plot trajectory on contours
Experiment: observe trajectory behaviour near saddle points vs minima

slots.label: Session 5 — Needham Ch. 3
slots.description: Read Needham Ch. 3 on **curvature** in the plane. Needham introduces curvature as the rate at which the tangent vector rotates — a beautifully intuitive approach. This sets up the generalisation to surface curvature next phase.

subtasks.label: Read Needham Ch. 3 (curvature of plane curves)
Work through at least 3 of the exercises

weeks.title: Inner Products, Metrics, and Coordinate-Free Thinking

slots.label: Session 1 — Inner Products and Metrics
slots.description:
Study **inner product spaces**, bilinear forms, and the concept of a **metric** as a way to measure lengths and angles. The standard Euclidean metric gᵢⱼ = δᵢⱼ. Non-standard metrics: how changing the inner product changes geometry.

**Connection:** In parameter space, the 'natural' metric isn't Euclidean — it's the **Fisher information metric**, where distances reflect statistical distinguishability. Natural gradient descent uses this non-Euclidean metric instead of the Euclidean one.

subtasks.label: Study inner product axioms and bilinear forms
Compute lengths and angles under a non-standard metric
Read about Fisher information metric (conceptual overview only)

slots.label: Session 2 — Coordinate Systems and Change of Coordinates
slots.description:
Study how vectors, matrices, and especially **metrics** transform under change of coordinates. The metric transforms as g' = JᵀgJ where J is the Jacobian of the coordinate change. This is your gateway to **tensor transformation laws**.

Practice: express the Euclidean metric in polar coordinates.

subtasks.label: Study how metric components transform under coordinate change
Compute the Euclidean metric in polar coordinates
Watch first 3 videos of Eigenchris 'Tensors for Beginners'

slots.label: Session 3 — Intro to Tensors
slots.description: A tensor is an object that transforms 'correctly' under coordinate changes. Study **covectors** (dual vectors, 1-forms), the distinction between upper and lower indices, and **tensor products**. You don't need the full machinery yet — just enough to read expressions like gᵢⱼ, Γⁱⱼₖ, Rⁱⱼₖₗ without panic.

subtasks.label: Study covectors / dual vectors / 1-forms
Understand upper vs lower indices and Einstein summation convention
Watch Eigenchris videos 4–8

slots.label: Session 4 — Metric Computations
slots.description:
Practice problems:
- Compute metric tensor in various coordinate systems (polar, spherical, cylindrical)
- Given a metric, compute lengths of curves, angles between vectors
- Implement: compute lengths of curves numerically under different metrics in Python

subtasks.label: Compute metric tensors in polar, spherical, and cylindrical coords
Compute the length of a curve on the sphere using the spherical metric
Code: numerically compute curve length under different metrics

slots.label: Session 5 — Foundations Review & Self-Assessment
slots.description:
Consolidation day. Review your notes from weeks 1–4. Try to write from memory:
- Spectral theorem statement
- Second-order Taylor expansion
- Implicit Function Theorem statement
- Metric transformation law

Identify gaps and revisit weak areas.

subtasks.label: Write spectral theorem statement from memory
Write second-order Taylor expansion from memory
Write IFT statement from memory
List 5 connections between foundations and loss landscapes
Identify and note your 3 weakest topics for targeted review

weeks.title: Buffer: Consolidation & Catch-Up

slots.label: Session 1 — Revisit Weakest Topics
slots.description: Return to the topics you flagged last Friday. Re-watch videos, redo exercises, or find alternative explanations. Don't move forward with shaky foundations.

subtasks.label: Revisit weak topic #1
Revisit weak topic #2

slots.label: Session 2 — Revisit Remaining Gaps
slots.description: Continue gap-filling. Try different resources if the original ones didn't click.

subtasks.label: Revisit weak topic #3 or explore alternative explanations
Try at least one problem from each weak area

slots.label: Session 3 — Phase 1 Comprehensive Problem Set
slots.description:
Work through a self-designed problem set covering all Phase 1 material. Time yourself.

- Eigendecomposition of a 3×3 symmetric matrix
- Compute Hessian and classify critical points of f(x,y,z)
- Compute metric in a non-standard coordinate system
- Apply IFT to find a local parametrisation

subtasks.label: Eigendecomposition problem
Hessian and critical point classification problem
Metric tensor computation problem
IFT application problem

slots.label: Session 4 — ML Integration: Loss Landscape Mini-Project
slots.description: Bring it all together: train a small CNN on MNIST, compute the Hessian (or top eigenvalues via Lanczos), visualise the loss surface using random 2D slices through parameter space. This preview will motivate everything in Phases 2–5.

subtasks.label: Train a small CNN on MNIST
Compute top eigenvalues of the Hessian using PyHessian or Lanczos iteration
Visualise 2D slice of loss surface (random directions in parameter space)
Journal: write observations connecting eigenvalues to what you see in the loss surface

slots.label: Session 5 — Phase 1 Reflection
slots.description: Write a 1-page reflection: what have you learned, what was hardest, what connections to your PhD are becoming clearer? Read ahead: skim Keenan Crane's DDG Lecture 1 to get excited about Phase 2.

subtasks.label: Write 1-page Phase 1 reflection
Skim DDG Lecture 1 preview

phases.title: Classical Differential Geometry: Curves and Surfaces
phases.description: The classical theory of curves and surfaces in ℝ³. You'll learn curvature, the first and second fundamental forms, Gaussian and mean curvature, and geodesics. Keenan Crane's DDG course is ideal — it's designed for CS students, uses computational thinking, and connects to discrete/computational geometry. This phase builds the geometric vocabulary you'll later generalise to abstract manifolds.
phases.bookChapters: Do Carmo – Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces, Ch. 1–4
phases.bookDescription: Do Carmo is the classic reference. It's more rigorous than Crane's lectures — use it as a parallel track for deeper proofs and exercises. Chapters 1–4 cover curves, regular surfaces, the Gauss map, and intrinsic geometry.
phases.completionNote: You can compute curvatures of curves and surfaces, understand what the first and second fundamental forms measure, know Gauss's Theorema Egregium (curvature is intrinsic), and understand geodesics as locally shortest paths. You can explain why the geometry of a loss surface is determined by its metric, not its embedding.

weeks.title: Curvature of Curves: Frenet–Serret, Arc Length, Torsion

slots.label: Session 1 — Curves, Arc Length, Curvature
slots.description: Watch DDG Lectures 1–2. Study **arc-length parametrisation**, **curvature** κ = |dT/ds| as the rate of turning of the tangent vector. The Frenet frame {T, N, B}.

subtasks.label: Watch DDG Lectures 1–2
Study arc-length parametrisation and curvature formula
Compute curvature for a helix and a parabola

slots.label: Session 2 — Frenet–Serret Frame and Torsion
slots.description: Study the full **Frenet–Serret apparatus**: T, N, B, curvature κ, and torsion τ. The Frenet equations describe how the frame evolves along the curve. Torsion measures how much the curve twists out of its osculating plane.

subtasks.label: Write down and understand the 3 Frenet–Serret equations
Compute κ and τ for a helix
Understand: torsion measures deviation from planarity

slots.label: Session 3 — DDG Lectures 3–4
slots.description: Continue with DDG. Lecture 3 covers **exterior algebra** (a way to think about areas and volumes algebraically). Lecture 4 covers **differential forms on surfaces**. These may feel abstract — that's okay. Focus on the geometric intuition Crane provides.

subtasks.label: Watch DDG Lecture 3 (exterior algebra)
Watch DDG Lecture 4 (differential forms)
Note: what is a k-form intuitively?

slots.label: Session 4 — Curve Computations
slots.description: Compute curvature and torsion for several curves. Plot curves with their Frenet frames in 3D using matplotlib.

subtasks.label: Compute κ and τ for 3 different space curves
Plot a curve in 3D with Frenet frame vectors overlaid
Do Carmo Ch. 1 exercises: at least 4 problems

slots.label: Session 5 — Do Carmo Ch. 1
slots.description: Read Do Carmo Ch. 1 alongside Needham's treatment of curvature. Compare the two approaches — Do Carmo is formal, Needham is visual. Both are valuable.

subtasks.label: Read Do Carmo Ch. 1 (curves)
Compare Do Carmo's and Needham's approaches to curvature

weeks.title: Surfaces: First Fundamental Form (Metric on Surfaces)

slots.label: Session 1 — Regular Surfaces and the First Fundamental Form
slots.description:
Study **regular surfaces** in ℝ³ and the **first fundamental form** I = E du² + 2F du dv + G dv². This is the **metric** induced on the surface from the ambient ℝ³. It lets you compute lengths, angles, and areas intrinsically on the surface.

**Connection:** The parameter space of a neural network is a high-dimensional surface. The Fisher information metric is the natural 'first fundamental form' on this statistical manifold. Standard gradient descent uses the Euclidean metric; natural gradient descent uses the Fisher metric.

subtasks.label: Watch DDG Lecture 5
Derive the first fundamental form for the sphere and the torus
Compute E, F, G for 2 parametrised surfaces

slots.label: Session 2 — Lengths, Angles, Areas from the Metric
slots.description: Use the first fundamental form to compute **arc lengths of curves on surfaces**, **angles between curves**, and **areas of regions**. These are all determined by E, F, G — you don't need to know how the surface sits in 3D, only the metric.

subtasks.label: Compute length of a curve on the sphere using the first fundamental form
Compute area of a region on a surface
Prove: angle between coordinate curves is cos θ = F/√(EG)

slots.label: Session 3 — Isometries and Intrinsic Geometry
slots.description:
An **isometry** preserves the first fundamental form (metric). Study: when are two surfaces isometric? A cylinder is isometric to a plane (you can unroll it). A sphere is NOT isometric to a plane (you can't flatten a globe without distortion). The metric captures all intrinsic geometry.

**Connection:** Reparametrising a neural network (changing coordinates in weight space) is an isometry of the parameter manifold if the metric is preserved. Different parametrisations of the same model can have very different Euclidean geometry but the same Riemannian geometry.

subtasks.label: Prove: a cylinder is locally isometric to a plane
Understand why a sphere cannot be isometric to a plane
Define isometry and local isometry formally

slots.label: Session 4 — First Fundamental Form Computations
slots.description: Practice computing E, F, G for various surfaces. Compute lengths, areas, and check isometry conditions. Do Carmo Ch. 2 exercises.

subtasks.label: Compute first fundamental form for 4 surfaces (including non-trivial ones)
Do Carmo Ch. 2 exercises: at least 5 problems
Numerically compute geodesic distances on a surface in Python

slots.label: Session 5 — Do Carmo Ch. 2
slots.description: Read Do Carmo Ch. 2 (Regular Surfaces). This gives the rigorous foundation for everything you've been studying computationally.

subtasks.label: Read Do Carmo Ch. 2 (first half)
Read Do Carmo Ch. 2 (second half)

weeks.title: Gauss Map, Second Fundamental Form, Curvature of Surfaces

slots.label: Session 1 — The Gauss Map and Shape Operator
slots.description: The **Gauss map** N: S → S² assigns to each point its unit normal. The **shape operator** (or Weingarten map) dN measures how the normal changes — this is the key to surface curvature. The shape operator is a linear map on the tangent plane.

subtasks.label: Watch DDG Lecture 6
Define the Gauss map and shape operator
Compute shape operator for a sphere and a cylinder

slots.label: Session 2 — Principal, Gaussian, and Mean Curvature
slots.description:
The eigenvalues of the shape operator are the **principal curvatures** κ₁, κ₂. **Gaussian curvature** K = κ₁κ₂ (product). **Mean curvature** H = (κ₁+κ₂)/2 (average). The **second fundamental form** II = e du² + 2f du dv + g dv² encodes this.

**Connection:** Gaussian curvature is analogous to the product of Hessian eigenvalues (the determinant of the Hessian). At a saddle point, the Hessian has eigenvalues of opposite sign → negative determinant. On a surface, a saddle has K < 0. The analogy is deep and becomes exact in the right setting.

subtasks.label: Watch DDG Lecture 7
Compute K and H for sphere, cylinder, and saddle surface z = xy
Understand: K > 0 (bowl), K < 0 (saddle), K = 0 (flat/cylindrical)
Write the analogy: Gaussian curvature ↔ determinant of Hessian

slots.label: Session 3 — Gauss's Theorema Egregium
slots.description:
**Theorema Egregium** (Remarkable Theorem): Gaussian curvature K depends only on the first fundamental form — it's **intrinsic**! You don't need to know how the surface sits in 3D. This is one of the most beautiful results in mathematics.

**Connection:** This means curvature of the loss landscape is an intrinsic property of the parameter manifold with its metric. It doesn't depend on how you embed or visualise the landscape — it's a property of the geometry itself.

subtasks.label: Study the statement of Theorema Egregium
Understand the proof sketch: K can be expressed using only E, F, G and their derivatives
Reflect: why is this 'remarkable'? What does it mean for loss landscapes?

slots.label: Session 4 — Curvature Computations
slots.description: Compute principal curvatures, Gaussian curvature, and mean curvature for various surfaces. Visualise curvature on surfaces using colour maps.

subtasks.label: Compute K and H for 4 surfaces by hand
Code: colour-map Gaussian curvature on a mesh surface
Do Carmo Ch. 3 exercises: at least 4 problems

slots.label: Session 5 — Do Carmo Ch. 3 (Gauss Map)
slots.description: Read Do Carmo Ch. 3 for the full rigorous treatment of the Gauss map and curvature.

subtasks.label: Read Do Carmo Ch. 3
Work through Do Carmo's proof of Theorema Egregium

weeks.title: Geodesics and Intrinsic Geometry of Surfaces

slots.label: Session 1 — Geodesics: Shortest Paths on Surfaces
slots.description:
A **geodesic** is a curve on a surface with zero geodesic curvature — it's the surface analogue of a straight line. Study the geodesic equation: the acceleration is always normal to the surface.

**Connection:** In optimization on manifolds, the natural analogue of 'gradient descent in a straight line' is 'follow a geodesic'. The geodesic equation on the loss landscape with the Fisher metric gives natural gradient descent.

subtasks.label: Watch DDG Lecture 8
Derive the geodesic equation on a surface
Find geodesics on the sphere (great circles) and the cylinder

slots.label: Session 2 — Geodesics continued; Parallel Transport
slots.description: Study **parallel transport**: how to move a vector along a curve without 'turning' it (relative to the surface). The failure of parallel transport around a loop is related to curvature. Study the **Christoffel symbols** as the 'correction terms' for differentiating on a curved surface.

subtasks.label: Study parallel transport along curves on surfaces
Compute Christoffel symbols for the sphere
Understand: parallel transport around a closed loop on a sphere rotates a vector by the enclosed solid angle

slots.label: Session 3 — Gauss–Bonnet Theorem (Survey)
slots.description:
The **Gauss–Bonnet theorem** connects curvature to topology: ∫∫ K dA = 2π χ(S). The total Gaussian curvature of a closed surface equals 2π times its Euler characteristic. A sphere has χ = 2, a torus has χ = 0.

This is a survey — understand the statement and its meaning, don't worry about the full proof.

subtasks.label: Watch DDG Lecture 9
State the Gauss–Bonnet theorem and verify for the sphere
Understand: Euler characteristic and what it measures topologically

slots.label: Session 4 — Geodesic Computations
slots.description: Solve the geodesic equation numerically for several surfaces. Visualise geodesics and parallel transport.

subtasks.label: Numerically solve geodesic equation on a sphere and torus
Visualise parallel transport of a vector around a loop on a sphere
Do Carmo Ch. 4 exercises: at least 4 problems

slots.label: Session 5 — Do Carmo Ch. 4 + DDG Notes
slots.description: Read Do Carmo Ch. 4 (intrinsic geometry, geodesics, Gauss–Bonnet). Also skim the corresponding sections in Crane's DDG notes PDF.

subtasks.label: Read Do Carmo Ch. 4
Skim corresponding DDG notes sections

weeks.title: Buffer: Phase 2 Consolidation

slots.label: Session 1 — Review: Curvature Concepts
slots.description: Review principal curvatures, Gaussian curvature, mean curvature, Theorema Egregium. Try to state each concept from memory, then check.

subtasks.label: From memory: define principal curvatures, K, H
From memory: state Theorema Egregium
From memory: write geodesic equation

slots.label: Session 2 — Comprehensive Problem Set
slots.description: Timed problem set covering all Phase 2 material. Include curvature computation, geodesics, parallel transport, and Gauss–Bonnet.

subtasks.label: Curvature computation problems (30 min)
Geodesic problems (30 min)
Gauss–Bonnet application problem (20 min)
Review and correct mistakes (40 min)

slots.label: Session 3 — Catch-Up on Weak Areas
slots.description: Revisit topics that were difficult. Rewatch DDG lectures or reread Do Carmo sections as needed.

subtasks.label: Identify and revisit weakest 2 topics from Phase 2
Redo exercises from those topics

slots.label: Session 4 — Loss Landscape Connection Mini-Project
slots.description: Use surface curvature concepts on a loss surface: take a 2D slice of a small network's loss landscape and compute approximate Gaussian curvature at the minimum. Compare to the Hessian eigenvalue analysis from Phase 1.

subtasks.label: Generate 2D loss surface slice from a trained small network
Approximate Gaussian curvature at the minimum numerically
Compare to Hessian eigenvalue analysis — do they agree?

slots.label: Session 5 — Phase 2 Reflection
slots.description: Write a reflection on Phase 2. What geometric intuitions have you built? How do curves and surfaces connect to your PhD?

subtasks.label: Write 1-page Phase 2 reflection
Preview Lee Ch. 1 for Phase 3

phases.title: Smooth Manifolds: Going Abstract
phases.course: Frederic Schuller – Lectures on Geometrical Anatomy of Theoretical Physics
phases.description: The leap from surfaces in ℝ³ to abstract manifolds. You'll learn coordinate charts, smooth maps, tangent spaces as derivations, cotangent spaces, vector fields, and differential forms. This is where the mathematics becomes powerful enough to describe the parameter space of a neural network as a genuine geometric object. Schuller's lectures are rigorous but clear, aimed at physicists — a good match for your applied goals.
phases.bookChapters: Lee – Introduction to Smooth Manifolds, Ch. 1–6, 11
phases.bookDescription: Lee is the gold standard textbook. It's thorough and well-written. Use it as reference alongside the lectures — you don't need to read every proof, but the definitions and key theorems are essential.
phases.completionNote: You can define a smooth manifold from charts, compute tangent vectors as derivations, work with covectors and differential forms, understand vector fields and their flows, and see parameter space as a manifold. You can read papers that say 'consider the manifold of network parameters with metric g'.

weeks.title: Topological & Smooth Manifolds, Charts, Atlases

slots.label: Session 1 — Topological Manifolds and Charts
slots.description:
A manifold is a space that **locally looks like ℝⁿ**. A **chart** (U, φ) is a homeomorphism from an open subset U of the manifold to an open subset of ℝⁿ. An **atlas** is a collection of charts covering the manifold. Study examples: sphere, torus, projective space.

**Connection:** The parameter space of a neural network with n weights is an open subset of ℝⁿ — it's already a manifold! But the interesting geometry comes from the metric we put on it, not from its trivial topology.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 1 (topology review)
Read Lee Ch. 1 §1.1–1.3 (topological manifolds, charts, atlases)
Define chart, atlas, and give 3 examples of manifolds with explicit charts

slots.label: Session 2 — Smooth Structures and Smooth Maps
slots.description: A **smooth structure** is a maximal atlas where all transition maps φⱼ ∘ φᵢ⁻¹ are smooth (C∞). A **smooth map** between manifolds is smooth in every chart. Study the transition map / compatibility condition carefully — this is the foundation of everything.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 2
Read Lee Ch. 1 §1.4–1.6 (smooth structures, smooth maps)
Compute transition maps for the sphere with stereographic projection charts

slots.label: Session 3 — Examples of Manifolds
slots.description: Study important manifold examples: **Lie groups** (GL(n), O(n), SO(n)), **projective spaces**, **Grassmannians**, **product manifolds**. Many of these appear in ML: orthogonal weight matrices live on O(n), low-rank weight matrices live on Grassmannians.

subtasks.label: Study Lie groups: GL(n), O(n), SO(n) as manifolds
Study Grassmannians (spaces of k-dimensional subspaces)
Note: where do these manifolds appear in ML/neural networks?

slots.label: Session 4 — Chart Computations
slots.description: Practice constructing charts, computing transition maps, and verifying smoothness. Lee Ch. 1 exercises.

subtasks.label: Construct explicit charts for RP² and verify transition map smoothness
Lee Ch. 1 exercises: at least 5 problems
Show that SO(n) is a manifold of dimension n(n-1)/2

slots.label: Session 5 — Lee Ch. 1 Deep Read
slots.description: Finish reading Lee Ch. 1. Focus on: partition of unity (a technical tool you'll need later), examples, and the key definitions.

subtasks.label: Finish Lee Ch. 1
Make a cheat sheet: all key definitions from Ch. 1

weeks.title: Tangent Spaces, Tangent Bundle, Pushforward

slots.label: Session 1 — Tangent Vectors as Derivations
slots.description:
On an abstract manifold, a **tangent vector** at p is a derivation: a linear map v: C∞(M) → ℝ satisfying the Leibniz rule. In coordinates, v = vⁱ ∂/∂xⁱ. The **tangent space** TₚM is the vector space of all tangent vectors at p.

This is the hardest conceptual leap. Spend time on it.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 5
Read Lee Ch. 3 (tangent vectors)
Verify: ∂/∂xⁱ are derivations and form a basis for TₚM
Write in your own words: what IS a tangent vector on an abstract manifold?

slots.label: Session 2 — The Pushforward (Differential of a Map)
slots.description: If F: M → N is smooth, the **pushforward** (or differential) dF_p: TₚM → T_{F(p)}N maps tangent vectors forward. In coordinates, dF is represented by the Jacobian matrix. This generalises the Jacobian from Phase 1 to manifolds.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 6
Compute pushforward of the inclusion S² ↪ ℝ³
Verify: in coordinates, pushforward = Jacobian matrix

slots.label: Session 3 — Tangent Bundle and Vector Fields
slots.description:
The **tangent bundle** TM = ⋃ TₚM is itself a manifold (of dimension 2n). A **vector field** is a smooth assignment of a tangent vector to each point: X: M → TM. Vector fields generate **flows** — families of diffeomorphisms.

**Connection:** The gradient vector field ∇L on the parameter manifold generates the gradient flow — the continuous-time limit of gradient descent. The geometry of this flow is determined by the metric.

subtasks.label: Define tangent bundle and verify it's a 2n-dimensional manifold
Define vector field and flow
Understand: gradient descent as following the flow of -∇L

slots.label: Session 4 — Tangent Space Exercises
slots.description: Lee Ch. 3 exercises. Practice computing tangent spaces, pushforwards, and working with vector fields in coordinates.

subtasks.label: Lee Ch. 3 exercises: at least 5 problems
Compute tangent space of SO(3) at the identity
Compute the pushforward of a rotation map on S²

slots.label: Session 5 — Lee Ch. 3–4
slots.description: Read Lee Ch. 3–4 (tangent vectors, submersions/immersions/embeddings).

subtasks.label: Read Lee Ch. 3
Skim Lee Ch. 4 (immersions, submersions)

weeks.title: Cotangent Spaces, Differential Forms, Pullback

slots.label: Session 1 — Cotangent Space and 1-Forms
slots.description:
The **cotangent space** T*ₚM is the dual of TₚM. A **covector** (1-form) at p eats a tangent vector and returns a number. In coordinates, the basis is {dxⁱ}. The **differential** df of a function is a 1-form: df(v) = v(f).

**Connection:** The gradient of the loss function is naturally a covector (1-form), not a vector! To turn it into a vector (for gradient descent), you need a metric. Different metrics give different 'gradient vectors' — this is the heart of natural gradient.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 7
Read Lee Ch. 6 (cotangent bundle)
Compute df in coordinates for several functions
Understand: gradient is a covector; the metric converts it to a vector

slots.label: Session 2 — Differential k-Forms and Exterior Derivative
slots.description:
Study **k-forms** as antisymmetric multilinear maps on tangent vectors. The **exterior derivative** d generalises the gradient, curl, and divergence. Key property: d² = 0.

Focus on 1-forms and 2-forms — you don't need the full generality for loss landscape applications, but understanding the structure helps.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 8
Define k-forms and wedge product
Compute exterior derivatives of several 1-forms

slots.label: Session 3 — Pullback of Forms
slots.description: If F: M → N, the **pullback** F* pulls differential forms back from N to M: (F*ω)(v₁,...,vₖ) = ω(dF(v₁),...,dF(vₖ)). Key property: pullback commutes with exterior derivative. This is the dual of the pushforward.

subtasks.label: Define pullback of differential forms
Compute pullbacks of 1-forms under several maps
Verify: F*(dω) = d(F*ω) for an example

slots.label: Session 4 — Forms and Derivatives Exercises
slots.description: Lee Ch. 6 & 11 exercises. Practice with differential forms, exterior derivatives, and pullbacks.

subtasks.label: Lee Ch. 6 exercises: at least 4 problems
Lee Ch. 11 exercises: at least 3 problems
Verify Stokes' theorem for a simple case

slots.label: Session 5 — Lee Ch. 5–6 + Needham Parallel
slots.description: Read Lee Ch. 5 (submanifolds) and Ch. 6 (cotangent bundle). Optionally read Needham's treatment of forms for visual intuition.

subtasks.label: Read Lee Ch. 5 (submanifolds)
Read Lee Ch. 6 (cotangent bundle, differential forms intro)

weeks.title: Buffer: Phase 3 Consolidation & Manifold Fluency

slots.label: Session 1 — Manifold Fluency Drill
slots.description: From memory, define: smooth manifold, tangent space, pushforward, cotangent space, differential form, pullback. Then check against Lee. Identify gaps.

subtasks.label: Write all key definitions from memory
Check against Lee and note mistakes
Revisit weakest definitions/concepts

slots.label: Session 2 — Catch-Up
slots.description: Revisit anything that's still unclear from weeks 11–13. Focus on the conceptual leaps (tangent vectors as derivations, forms as dual objects).

subtasks.label: Revisit the 2 hardest topics from Phase 3
Find and work through alternative explanations

slots.label: Session 3 — Mixed Problem Set
slots.description: Comprehensive problems mixing Phase 2 and Phase 3 material. This ensures the abstract manifold concepts connect to the concrete surface geometry.

subtasks.label: Compute tangent space and pushforward on a concrete surface
Express the first fundamental form as a Riemannian metric in charts
Compute a pullback for a concrete map between surfaces

slots.label: Session 4 — Read Ahead: Riemannian Metrics
slots.description: Preview Lee Ch. 13 (Riemannian Metrics) to prepare for Phase 4. Just read the definitions and examples — don't worry about proofs yet.

subtasks.label: Skim Lee Ch. 13 §13.1–13.2
Note: how does the abstract Riemannian metric relate to the first fundamental form?

slots.label: Session 5 — Phase 3 Reflection
slots.description: Write your Phase 3 reflection. The key insight: differential geometry on abstract manifolds is about defining geometric concepts (length, angle, curvature) using only smooth structure + metric, without reference to any ambient space.

subtasks.label: Write 1-page Phase 3 reflection
List all connections to loss landscapes discovered so far

phases.title: Riemannian Geometry: Metrics, Connections, Curvature
phases.description: The core of the plan. Riemannian geometry equips a smooth manifold with a metric tensor, enabling measurement of distances, angles, and curvature. You'll learn the Levi-Civita connection, geodesics on abstract manifolds, Riemann curvature tensor, Ricci curvature, and sectional curvature. This is exactly the machinery needed to rigorously study loss landscape geometry.
phases.bookChapters: Lee – Introduction to Smooth Manifolds, Ch. 13 + Lee – Riemannian Manifolds (Ch. 1–7) or Do Carmo – Riemannian Geometry (Ch. 1–4)
phases.bookDescription: Lee's Riemannian Manifolds is concise and clear. Do Carmo's Riemannian Geometry is the classic. Either works well. For Absil's optimization perspective, start reading chapters 1–3 during this phase.
phases.completionNote: You can define and compute Riemannian metrics, Christoffel symbols, covariant derivatives, geodesics, and curvature tensors on abstract manifolds. You understand the Levi-Civita connection as 'the' natural way to differentiate on a Riemannian manifold. You can explain the geometric content of the Fisher information metric and natural gradient descent.

weeks.title: Riemannian Metrics: The Inner Product on Tangent Spaces

slots.label: Session 1 — Riemannian Metric Definition
slots.description:
A **Riemannian metric** g is a smoothly varying inner product on each tangent space: gₚ: TₚM × TₚM → ℝ. In coordinates, g = gᵢⱼ dxⁱ ⊗ dxʲ. This generalises the first fundamental form to abstract manifolds.

**Connection:** The Fisher information matrix F(θ) = E[∇log p(x|θ) ∇log p(x|θ)ᵀ] is a Riemannian metric on the parameter manifold. It measures how much the output distribution changes per unit parameter change.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 10
Define Riemannian metric formally
Compute the Riemannian metric on S² in spherical coordinates
Write down the Fisher information metric for a Gaussian distribution

slots.label: Session 2 — Riemannian Distance, Volume, Gradient
slots.description:
With a metric, define **Riemannian distance** d(p,q) = inf of path lengths, **Riemannian volume form**, and the **Riemannian gradient**: (grad f)ⁱ = gⁱʲ ∂f/∂xʲ. The metric 'raises the index' of df to get a vector.

**Connection:** The natural gradient is (F(θ))⁻¹ ∇L(θ) — this is exactly the Riemannian gradient with the Fisher metric! Standard gradient descent uses the Euclidean metric (gⁱʲ = δⁱʲ).

subtasks.label: Define Riemannian distance and show it's a metric space
Derive the Riemannian gradient formula: (grad f)ⁱ = gⁱʲ ∂ⱼf
Verify: natural gradient = Riemannian gradient with Fisher metric
Compute Riemannian gradient of a function on the sphere

slots.label: Session 3 — Musical Isomorphisms and Index Gymnastics
slots.description: The metric lets you 'raise and lower indices': convert between vectors and covectors. The **flat** ♭ map sends v → g(v,·) and the **sharp** ♯ map is its inverse. Practice index notation and Einstein summation.

subtasks.label: Define musical isomorphisms ♭ and ♯
Practice raising and lowering indices on 5 examples
Rewrite the Riemannian gradient formula using musical isomorphisms

slots.label: Session 4 — Natural Gradient Implementation
slots.description:
Implement natural gradient descent for a simple model. Compare convergence of standard GD vs natural GD on a toy problem.

- Implement for a 2-parameter Gaussian model
- Compute Fisher information matrix analytically
- Run both standard and natural gradient descent
- Plot convergence comparison

links.text: Amari: Natural Gradient Works Efficiently in Learning (paper)

subtasks.label: Compute Fisher information matrix for a 2-parameter Gaussian
Implement standard gradient descent
Implement natural gradient descent
Plot and compare convergence trajectories

slots.label: Session 5 — Lee Riemannian Ch. 1 + Absil Ch. 1
slots.description: Read the introduction chapters of both Lee's Riemannian Manifolds and Absil's Optimization Algorithms on Matrix Manifolds. Absil gives the optimization perspective you need for your PhD.

subtasks.label: Read Lee Riemannian Manifolds Ch. 1
Read Absil Ch. 1 (intro to optimization on manifolds)

weeks.title: Connections and Covariant Differentiation

slots.label: Session 1 — The Problem of Differentiating Vector Fields
slots.description: On a curved manifold, you can't just subtract tangent vectors at different points (they live in different vector spaces). You need a **connection**: a rule for 'transporting' vectors from one tangent space to another. The **covariant derivative** ∇_X Y generalises the directional derivative to vector fields on manifolds.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 11
Understand: why can't you just use partial derivatives on a manifold?
Define an affine connection / covariant derivative axiomatically

slots.label: Session 2 — Levi-Civita Connection and Christoffel Symbols
slots.description: The **Levi-Civita connection** is THE unique connection that is (1) torsion-free and (2) metric-compatible. The **Christoffel symbols** Γⁱⱼₖ = ½ gⁱˡ(∂ⱼgₖˡ + ∂ₖgⱼˡ − ∂ˡgⱼₖ) encode the connection in coordinates.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 12
Derive the Christoffel symbol formula from metric compatibility + torsion-free
Compute Christoffel symbols for S² in spherical coordinates

slots.label: Session 3 — Geodesics on Riemannian Manifolds
slots.description: Geodesics are curves with zero covariant acceleration: ∇_γ' γ' = 0. In coordinates: d²xⁱ/dt² + Γⁱⱼₖ (dxʲ/dt)(dxᵏ/dt) = 0. This generalises 'straight lines' to curved spaces. The **exponential map** exp_p: TₚM → M sends tangent vectors to geodesics.

subtasks.label: Derive the geodesic equation from ∇_γ' γ' = 0
Solve the geodesic equation for the sphere
Define the exponential map and compute it for a simple example

slots.label: Session 4 — Christoffel Symbol Computations
slots.description: Compute Christoffel symbols and geodesic equations for several manifolds. This is unavoidably computational — embrace it.

subtasks.label: Compute Christoffel symbols for the Poincaré half-plane
Compute geodesic equation for the torus
Numerically solve geodesic equations and plot geodesics on surfaces

slots.label: Session 5 — Lee Riemannian Ch. 2–4 + Absil Ch. 2
slots.description: Read about connections and geodesics in Lee's Riemannian Manifolds. Read Absil Ch. 2 on retractions — the computational approximation to the exponential map used in practical optimization.

subtasks.label: Read Lee Riemannian Ch. 2–4 (connections, geodesics)
Read Absil Ch. 2 (retractions)

weeks.title: Curvature Tensors: Riemann, Ricci, Scalar

slots.label: Session 1 — Riemann Curvature Tensor
slots.description:
The **Riemann curvature tensor** R(X,Y)Z = ∇_X ∇_Y Z − ∇_Y ∇_X Z − ∇_[X,Y] Z measures the failure of covariant derivatives to commute. In components: Rⁱⱼₖₗ. This is the master curvature object — all other curvatures derive from it.

**Connection:** The Riemann tensor captures the full curvature of parameter space. Its traces (Ricci and scalar curvature) provide summary statistics of the curvature. These are relevant for understanding the geometry of the loss landscape at different scales.

subtasks.label: Watch Schuller Lecture 13
Define the Riemann curvature tensor
Compute R for the sphere in spherical coordinates
Understand: R = 0 means 'flat' (parallel transport is path-independent)

slots.label: Session 2 — Ricci Curvature and Scalar Curvature
slots.description: **Ricci curvature** Rᵢⱼ = Rᵏᵢₖⱼ is a trace of the Riemann tensor. **Scalar curvature** R = gⁱʲRᵢⱼ is the full trace. **Sectional curvature** K(σ) measures curvature in a 2-plane σ ⊂ TₚM. For surfaces, sectional curvature = Gaussian curvature.

subtasks.label: Define Ricci curvature and scalar curvature
Define sectional curvature
Verify: for 2D surfaces, sectional curvature = Gaussian curvature
Compute Ricci and scalar curvature for the sphere

slots.label: Session 3 — Curvature and Geodesic Deviation
slots.description:
Curvature controls how nearby geodesics diverge or converge: the **Jacobi equation**. Positive curvature → geodesics converge (like on a sphere). Negative curvature → geodesics diverge (like on a saddle).

**Connection:** In optimization, this means: in positively curved regions of the loss landscape, gradient descent trajectories starting nearby will converge. In negatively curved regions, they'll diverge. This has implications for the stability and sensitivity of training.

subtasks.label: Study the Jacobi equation for geodesic deviation
Understand: positive curvature → focusing, negative → defocusing
Write a paragraph connecting geodesic deviation to optimizer trajectory stability

slots.label: Session 4 — Curvature Computations
slots.description: Compute Riemann tensor, Ricci tensor, and scalar curvature for the Poincaré half-plane and the sphere. This is the hardest computational exercise in the plan — take your time.

subtasks.label: Compute full Riemann tensor for the Poincaré half-plane
Compute Ricci and scalar curvature from it
Verify results agree with known values (constant negative curvature)

slots.label: Session 5 — Lee Riemannian Ch. 5–7
slots.description: Read Lee's chapters on curvature. Focus on the definitions and key theorems, especially the symmetries of the Riemann tensor.

subtasks.label: Read Lee Riemannian Ch. 5–7 (curvature chapters)
Note the symmetries of Rᵢⱼₖₗ

weeks.title: Buffer: Phase 4 Consolidation

slots.label: Session 1 — Full Riemannian Geometry Review
slots.description: Write from memory: Riemannian metric, Christoffel symbols, geodesic equation, Riemann tensor, Ricci tensor, scalar curvature. Draw the conceptual dependency diagram: metric → connection → curvature.

subtasks.label: Write all key formulas from memory
Draw the dependency chain: metric → connection → curvature
Check against references and correct

slots.label: Session 2 — Comprehensive Riemannian Problem Set
slots.description: Full timed problem set: given a metric, compute Christoffel symbols, geodesics, Riemann tensor, Ricci tensor, scalar curvature.

subtasks.label: Given a 2D metric: full curvature computation pipeline
Geodesic computation for a given metric
Review and self-assess

slots.label: Session 3 — Optimization on Manifolds Code
slots.description: Implement Riemannian gradient descent on a simple manifold (e.g., the sphere or Stiefel manifold) using Absil's retraction approach. Compare to unconstrained optimization.

subtasks.label: Implement Riemannian gradient descent on the sphere
Use geoopt library for a more complex example
Compare Riemannian vs Euclidean optimization on a toy problem

slots.label: Session 4 — Absil Ch. 3–4
slots.description: Read Absil Ch. 3–4 on line-search and trust-region methods on manifolds. These are the practical optimisation algorithms you might use.

subtasks.label: Read Absil Ch. 3 (line-search on manifolds)
Read Absil Ch. 4 (trust-region on manifolds)

slots.label: Session 5 — Phase 4 Reflection + Paper Preview
slots.description: Write Phase 4 reflection. Then read the abstract and introduction of Li et al. 'Visualizing the Loss Landscape of Neural Nets' — you now have the mathematical vocabulary to understand the core ideas.

links.text: Li et al. – Visualizing the Loss Landscape (2018)

subtasks.label: Write 1-page Phase 4 reflection
Read Li et al. abstract + introduction

phases.title: Applications: Loss Landscapes, Information Geometry, and Neural Network Geometry
phases.description: Apply everything to your PhD research. This phase is paper-driven: you'll read and deeply understand the key papers on loss landscape geometry, information geometry, Hessian analysis, and the topology of loss surfaces. You'll implement the techniques from these papers and connect them to the differential geometry you've learned.
phases.bookChapters: Amari – Information Geometry and Its Applications, selected chapters
phases.bookDescription: Amari invented information geometry. His book is the definitive reference for the Fisher metric, natural gradient, and statistical manifolds. Read selected chapters relevant to neural networks.
phases.completionNote: You can read and understand loss landscape papers that use differential geometry. You can compute and interpret curvature quantities on the parameter manifold. You can explain the geometric content of the Fisher information metric, natural gradient, Hessian spectrum analysis, and mode connectivity. You have a strong foundation for your PhD research in neural network interpretability through the lens of differential geometry.

weeks.title: Information Geometry: Fisher Metric and Statistical Manifolds

slots.label: Session 1 — Statistical Manifolds
slots.description:
A **statistical manifold** is a manifold of probability distributions parametrised by θ. The **Fisher information metric** gᵢⱼ(θ) = E[∂ᵢ log p(x|θ) ∂ⱼ log p(x|θ)] is the natural Riemannian metric. It measures the 'statistical distance' between nearby distributions.

This is the central bridge between differential geometry and neural networks.

subtasks.label: Define statistical manifold and Fisher information metric
Compute Fisher metric for: Bernoulli, Gaussian, multinomial
Read Amari Ch. 1–2 (selected sections)

slots.label: Session 2 — Natural Gradient: The Full Picture
slots.description: Revisit **natural gradient descent** θ ← θ − η F⁻¹ ∇L with full geometric understanding. F⁻¹ ∇L is the Riemannian gradient with the Fisher metric. Study its properties: reparametrisation invariance, connection to second-order methods (Fisher ≈ Hessian for certain losses).

subtasks.label: Derive natural gradient as Riemannian gradient
Prove: natural gradient is reparametrisation-invariant
Understand: Fisher ≈ E[Hessian] for log-likelihood losses

slots.label: Session 3 — α-Connections and Dually Flat Manifolds
slots.description: Amari's key contribution: statistical manifolds have a family of **α-connections**, not just the Levi-Civita connection. The 1-connection and −1-connection give a **dually flat structure**. This is advanced — focus on understanding the concept, not the full formalism.

subtasks.label: Read about α-connections (Amari Ch. 3)
Understand dually flat manifolds at a conceptual level
Note: how does duality relate to exponential families?

slots.label: Session 4 — Fisher Metric Computations for Neural Networks
slots.description:
Compute/approximate the Fisher information metric for a small neural network. Explore the empirical Fisher (using samples) vs the exact Fisher.

Use PyTorch to compute the Fisher for a tiny MLP on MNIST.

subtasks.label: Implement empirical Fisher information matrix computation
Compute Fisher for a tiny MLP
Visualise Fisher matrix eigenvalue spectrum
Compare Fisher eigenvalues to Hessian eigenvalues

slots.label: Session 5 — Amari Chapters + Survey
slots.description: Read selected chapters of Amari's book. Also read a survey of information geometry applications in ML.

subtasks.label: Read Amari selected chapters (Ch. 1–3, key sections)
Find and read a recent survey on information geometry in deep learning

weeks.title: Loss Landscape Geometry: Hessian Analysis and Visualisation

slots.label: Session 1 — Paper: Li et al. 'Visualizing the Loss Landscape'
slots.description:
Deep read of Li et al. (2018). Key ideas: filter-normalised random directions for 2D loss surface slices, 1D linear interpolation between minima, the effect of skip connections on loss landscape smoothness.

Now you can understand WHY filter normalisation works: it accounts for the scale invariance of neural network parameters.

links.text: Li et al. – Visualizing the Loss Landscape

subtasks.label: Read Li et al. in full
Understand filter normalisation and its geometric justification
Note: what are the limitations of 2D slices?

slots.label: Session 2 — Paper: Sagun et al. 'Hessian of Over-Parametrized NNs'
slots.description:
Read Sagun et al. (2017). Key findings: the Hessian spectrum of neural networks has a bulk of near-zero eigenvalues and a few large outliers. This means the loss landscape is nearly flat in most directions and curved in only a few.

Connect to the Riemannian geometry: this means the effective dimensionality of the loss landscape is much lower than the number of parameters.

links.text: Sagun et al. – Empirical Analysis of Hessian

subtasks.label: Read Sagun et al. in full
Connect Hessian spectrum to the geometry: flat directions, curved directions
Understand: implications for effective dimensionality

slots.label: Session 3 — Paper: Dauphin et al. 'Saddle Point Problem'
slots.description: Read Dauphin et al. (2014). Key insight: in high-dimensional loss landscapes, local minima are rare; saddle points dominate. This is because at a critical point, each Hessian eigenvalue is independently positive or negative — in high dimensions, it's exponentially unlikely for ALL to be positive.

links.text: Dauphin et al. – Identifying and Attacking the Saddle Point Problem

subtasks.label: Read Dauphin et al. in full
Understand the probabilistic argument for saddle point dominance
Connect to differential geometry: saddle points have indefinite Hessian (mixed curvature)

slots.label: Session 4 — Reproduce Li et al. Visualisations
slots.description:
Reproduce the key visualisations from Li et al.:
- Train ResNet/VGG on CIFAR-10
- Generate filter-normalised random directions
- Plot 1D and 2D loss landscape slices
- Compare architectures with and without skip connections

subtasks.label: Set up Li et al. code or implement from scratch
Generate 2D loss surface visualisation for a trained model
Compare loss surfaces with/without skip connections

slots.label: Session 5 — Pennington & Worah + Additional Papers
slots.description: Read Pennington & Worah (2017) on random matrix theory applied to loss landscapes. Skim Eigencurve paper on measuring curvature along training trajectories.

links.text: Pennington & Worah – Random Matrix Theory for Loss Surfaces

subtasks.label: Read Pennington & Worah (key sections)
Skim Eigencurve paper

weeks.title: Mode Connectivity, Topology, and Loss Landscape Structure

slots.label: Session 1 — Mode Connectivity
slots.description:
Study **mode connectivity**: are different minima of the loss landscape connected by paths of low loss? Draxler et al. and Garipov et al. showed that minima found by SGD are typically connected by simple curves (not straight lines, but low-dimensional curves) in parameter space.

This is a topological statement about the loss landscape: the set of 'good' parameters is connected.

links.text: Garipov et al. – Loss Surfaces, Mode Connectivity (2018)
Draxler et al. – Essentially No Barriers in Neural Network Energy Landscape (2018)

subtasks.label: Read Garipov et al. (2018)
Read Draxler et al. (2018)
Understand: mode connectivity as a statement about loss landscape topology

slots.label: Session 2 — Topological Properties of Loss Landscapes
slots.description:
Read papers on the topology of loss landscapes. The loss function defines a filtration of parameter space: {θ : L(θ) ≤ c} for varying c. The topology of these sublevel sets (Betti numbers, connected components) reveals the structure of the landscape.

**Connection:** This connects Gauss–Bonnet and Morse theory (curvature → topology) to the loss landscape. Critical points of L with index k contribute to the k-th Betti number.

subtasks.label: Read Ballard et al. on topological properties
Study Morse theory at a conceptual level: critical points determine topology
Understand: Hessian index at critical points relates to Betti numbers

slots.label: Session 3 — Sharp vs Flat Minima Debate
slots.description:
The 'sharp vs flat minima' debate: Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, Keskar et al., and the Dinh et al. critique (reparametrisation can make any minimum arbitrarily sharp or flat). The resolution involves Riemannian geometry: you need a **reparametrisation-invariant** notion of sharpness.

This is where your differential geometry training pays off directly.

links.text: Keskar et al. – On Large-Batch Training (2017)
Dinh et al. – Sharp Minima Can Generalize (2017)

subtasks.label: Read Keskar et al. (sharp minima and large-batch training)
Read Dinh et al. critique (reparametrisation argument)
Understand: why reparametrisation-invariance requires Riemannian geometry

slots.label: Session 4 — Mode Connectivity Experiments
slots.description: Implement mode connectivity experiments: train a network from two different initialisations, find a low-loss curve connecting the two solutions using the method of Garipov et al.

subtasks.label: Train 2 networks from different initialisations
Implement Bezier curve or polychain fitting between solutions
Plot the loss along the connecting curve

slots.label: Session 5 — Physics Perspective + Additional Papers
slots.description: Read Frederick & Bhatt on the physics perspective of loss landscapes. Explore any additional papers relevant to your specific PhD research direction.

links.text: Frederick & Bhatt – Physics Perspective on Loss Landscape

subtasks.label: Read Frederick & Bhatt
Identify 2–3 papers most relevant to your specific PhD direction

weeks.title: Capstone Project: Geometric Analysis of a Loss Landscape

slots.label: Session 1 — Project Planning
slots.description:
Design your capstone project. Choose a model architecture and dataset. Plan which geometric analyses to run:
- Hessian spectrum analysis
- Loss landscape visualisation (Li et al. style)
- Fisher information metric computation
- Mode connectivity analysis
- Curvature along training trajectory

The goal is a comprehensive geometric portrait of one model's loss landscape.

subtasks.label: Choose model architecture and dataset
Write project plan: which analyses, what tools, expected insights
Set up code repository and experiment framework

slots.label: Session 2 — Training + Hessian Analysis
slots.description: Train the model. Compute Hessian eigenvalue spectrum at multiple points during training. Track how the spectrum evolves.

subtasks.label: Train the model, saving checkpoints
Compute Hessian top eigenvalues at each checkpoint
Plot eigenvalue evolution during training

slots.label: Session 3 — Loss Surface Visualisation
slots.description: Generate 2D loss landscape visualisations using filter-normalised random directions. Compare early vs late training checkpoints.

subtasks.label: Generate 2D loss slices at trained solution
Compare landscape at early vs late training
Generate high-resolution visualisations for the report

slots.label: Session 4 — Fisher Metric + Mode Connectivity
slots.description: Compute the empirical Fisher information metric. Run mode connectivity experiments between solutions from different seeds.

subtasks.label: Compute empirical Fisher and its eigenvalue spectrum
Compare Fisher spectrum to Hessian spectrum
Run mode connectivity between 2 trained solutions

slots.label: Session 5 — Analysis and Interpretation
slots.description: Bring together all results. Interpret them through the lens of differential geometry. What do the curvature quantities tell you about the loss landscape? How do they relate to generalisation?

subtasks.label: Compile all visualisations and data
Write geometric interpretation of each result
Identify surprising or interesting findings

weeks.title: Capstone Write-Up + Research Directions

slots.label: Session 1 — Write Capstone Report (Part 1)
slots.description: Begin writing a report (5–10 pages) summarising your capstone project. Structure: Introduction, Background (differential geometry summary), Methods, Results, Discussion, Future Directions.

subtasks.label: Write Introduction and Background sections
Write Methods section

slots.label: Session 2 — Write Capstone Report (Part 2)
slots.description: Continue writing: Results and Discussion sections.

subtasks.label: Write Results section with figures
Write Discussion section

slots.label: Session 3 — Future Research Directions
slots.description: Based on everything you've learned, identify 3–5 research directions where differential geometry could advance neural network interpretability. These could seed your PhD research proposals.

subtasks.label: Brainstorm 5–10 potential research directions
Narrow to top 3–5 and write a paragraph for each
Add Future Directions section to capstone report

slots.label: Session 4 — Polish Report + Create Reference Sheet
slots.description: Finish and polish the capstone report. Create a 2-page reference sheet of key differential geometry formulas and their loss landscape interpretations — this will be invaluable for your PhD.

subtasks.label: Polish and finalise capstone report
Create 2-page reference sheet: formulas + interpretations

slots.label: Session 5 — Build Reading List for Continued Study
slots.description: Curate a reading list for continuing beyond this plan. Include: advanced Riemannian geometry, geometric deep learning, information geometry, and cutting-edge loss landscape papers.

subtasks.label: Curate 15–20 papers/books for continued study
Organise by topic and priority

weeks.title: Final Buffer: Complete Review & Plan Forward

slots.label: Session 1 — Grand Review: Foundations → Applications
slots.description: Walk through the entire conceptual arc: linear algebra → multivariable calculus → curves & surfaces → abstract manifolds → Riemannian geometry → information geometry → loss landscapes. Write the dependency chain from memory.

subtasks.label: Write the full conceptual arc from memory
For each phase: state the 3 most important ideas
For each phase: state the key connection to loss landscapes

slots.label: Session 2 — Final Assessment
slots.description: Timed final assessment covering the full plan. This is for your own benefit — identify any remaining gaps before starting PhD research.

subtasks.label: Phase 1–2 questions (30 min)
Phase 3–4 questions (40 min)
Phase 5 application questions (30 min)
Review answers and identify gaps (20 min)

slots.label: Session 3 — Address Final Gaps
slots.description: Address any gaps identified in the final assessment. This is your last chance to solidify foundations before moving to active research.

subtasks.label: Revisit identified gaps
Work through additional exercises for weak areas

slots.label: Session 4 — Prepare PhD Research Toolkit
slots.description: Set up your ongoing research toolkit: code libraries (geoopt, PyHessian), reference sheets, paper collection, and a system for tracking geometric insights as you do research.

subtasks.label: Set up a clean code repository with your geometric analysis tools
Organise all reference sheets and notes
Set up a paper tracking system (Zotero/Notion/etc.)

slots.label: Session 5 — Celebration & Reflection
slots.description:
Write your final reflection. You've gone from CS foundations to Riemannian geometry and its applications to loss landscapes in 24 weeks. Celebrate the achievement and look forward to your research.

Consider sharing your capstone report with your PhD advisor.

subtasks.label: Write final reflection (1–2 pages)
Share capstone report with advisor
Celebrate completing the plan!
